title: Lamb tagine with prunes and almonds
subtitle: Moroccan sweet-savoury braised lamb
excerpt: Shoulder of lamb slow-cooked with prunes, blanched almonds, honey, cinnamon, and saffron until the meat is falling-soft and the sauce has reduced to a dark, sticky glaze. The sweet and savoury balance is the point: the prunes sweeten as they dissolve into the sauce, the almonds give crunch, the lamb gives depth. Eaten with couscous or khobz to scoop up the sauce.
sourceNotes: Mrouzia and lamb-with-prunes tagine are among the oldest Moroccan dishes in the culinary record, appearing in medieval Andalusian and Maghrebi manuscripts. The sweet-savoury combination of meat with dried fruits, honey, and cinnamon is characteristic of the Fez cooking tradition. Paula Wolfert's Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco is the primary English-language source.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. The sauce improves on reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Make 1 day ahead and reheat gently.

content.text: Lamb shoulder is the correct cut. It has the fat and connective tissue to withstand 2 hours of braising without drying out; leg will tighten up. Bone-in gives more flavour, but boneless shoulder is easier to serve and works just as well if you want convenience.

The prunes dissolve almost entirely during cooking, thickening and sweetening the sauce. Add the blanched almonds in the last 10 minutes — added earlier they go soft; added at the end they keep their texture and provide the crunch the dish needs.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large, heavy casserole over high heat. Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Brown the lamb in batches, 3 to 4 minutes per side, until deeply coloured. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} to the same pot and cook for 8 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{ras-el-hanout}} and cook for 2 minutes. Return the lamb. Add the saffron water and 300 ml of water. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 1 hour 15 minutes.

Add the {{prunes}} and {{honey}}. Stir and cook uncovered for a further 20 to 25 minutes until the prunes have softened and the sauce has reduced to a thick, dark glaze. In the last 10 minutes, stir in the {{blanched-almonds}}. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with couscous or flatbread.

items.symptom: Sauce too thin at the end
items.cause: Not reduced enough or too much water added initially
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 20 minutes and increase the heat to a strong simmer. The sauce should be thick enough to coat a spoon

items.symptom: Lamb still tough at 1 hour 30 minutes
items.cause: Cut too large or heat too low
items.fix: Shoulder needs low, steady heat — not a rolling boil. Continue cooking in 20-minute increments. The meat is done when it offers no resistance to a fork

items.cause: Prunes very sweet or too much honey
items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice to balance. The ratio should be sweet-savoury, not dessert-sweet

content.text: Lamb tagine with prunes and almonds is one of the great Moroccan celebration dishes. In Morocco it is made for Eid al-Adha and for wedding feasts, cooked slowly in an earthenware tagine pot over charcoal. The sweet-savoury register is entirely Moroccan and unlike most of the European cooking tradition — the combination of meat, dried fruit, honey, and warm spice traces its roots to medieval Arab cooking. In Britain it is a reliable dinner-party centrepiece that can be made entirely ahead of time and reheated.
